schools.summary: David's 'passionate and sinful sorrow' made him forget a king's duty, so that Joab had to recall him to it.
Matthew Henry: a prince's every act is watched; grief for so bad a son as Absalom was very unwise and unworthy.
Gill: the news reached Joab before David saw the king, so the general came braced to confront him.
Clarke frames the whole chapter — lament, Joab's threat, the recall, Shimei, Mephibosheth, Barzillai.
Gill: the people mourned too, their joy swallowed up in the king's sorrow.
Keil: the verse flows into the picture of shamed soldiers and the king crying with covered face.
Clarke: they mourned over their own success, seeing the king immoderately afflicted for a worthless son.
Gill: the army entered privately, one by one, not caring to be seen, as fearing the king's displeasure.
Wesley: they came secretly, ashamed, lest David look on them as sharers in killing his beloved son.
Gill: David wrapped himself in his mantle after the manner of mourners and would not thank his generals.
Clarke: the covered face was the custom of mourners; the repeated name is the universal language of lamentation, as in Virgil.
Clarke: Joab's speech shows insolence of office yet good sense; a good soldier but a bad man and dangerous subject.
Ellicott: with his hand red with Absalom's blood, Joab reproaches the father insolently yet counsels him as a loyal statesman.
Gill: Joab tracked David to his palace, where he kept up 'the same doleful ditty.'
Gill: the charge was not strictly true but had 'some appearance,' here greatly exaggerated.
Keil renders the Hebrew hyperbole: David loves his haters, and had Absalom lived and all died it would please him.
Wesley: not exactly true, but David's carriage gave it colour; such sharpness was needed to awaken him.
Joab was bound in David's interest to expostulate; 'speak to the heart' is the idiom for friendly, reassuring speech.
Gill: Joab took too much upon him, unbecoming a subject — the license of a powerful general over princes in that age.

debates.question: Is Joab's oath a threat of revolt or only a warning?

sides.position: an insolent overreach, a subject presuming beyond his place
no design of revolt, only a startling warning of real danger to rouse the king

themes: the harshest voice the truest friend

schools.summary: David yields, sits in the gate, and shows good-will; 'Israel had fled' means the beaten rebels, not the loyal, scattered home.
Gill: the gate was the public place of concourse where the king resumes his visibility.
Clarke: the gate was the place where justice was administered — David resumes his office there.
Matthew Henry: when convinced of a fault we must amend, though told of it by inferiors; timely yielding prevents worse.
The tribes' strife arose from a guilty conscience; the movement toward David was as yet without organisation or leadership.
Matthew Henry: David would return as an invited prince, not a conqueror — a type of the Son of David, who rules where invited.
Wesley: their renewed memory of David's merits was God's work, now reconciled to David.
Gill: all the tribes save Judah were at this strife over bringing the king back.

themes: the Son of David ruling where invited

schools.summary: The 'anointing' of Absalom is otherwise unrecorded and may be a loose form for 'appointed'; the delay was dangerous, and David seizes the moment.
Gill: the anointing was done really by Absalom's party or in effect by proclaiming him king.
Wesley: the people, their designs disappointed, now repented and were forward to bring David back.
Clarke: David dared not fall out with Judah, who held Jerusalem; he placed confidence in them to excite theirs, and dangled Amasa's command.
Ellicott: Judah was slowest because it was the cradle of the revolt and feared its reception; 'to his house' may be an accidental repetition.
Gill: David sent to Zadok and Abiathar, perhaps by their sons Ahimaaz and Jonathan.
Gill: as kinsmen David would deal gently and not be severe on Judah for their part in the conspiracy.
Ellicott: the Hebrew is 'bone' (cf. 2 Samuel 5:1); the moving point was that the king acknowledged the relationship.
Matthew Henry: the Son of David calls us brethren, his bone and flesh, encouraging hope of favour.
Ellicott: appointing the rebel general Amasa was bold but rash and unjust — a stroke to detach the rebel army and be rid of Joab.
Wesley: David gladly used Amasa to chastise Joab for murdering Absalom and to escape the bondage Joab held him in.
Gill: Amasa, Absalom's general, feared he would never be pardoned and counted his case desperate.

debates.question: Was the oath to Amasa wise or unjust?

sides.position: astute policy — it won the rebel soldiery and freed David from an overbearing general
rash and unjust — a traitor rewarded, and the seed of Amasa's later murder

schools.summary: Gill: David, not Amasa, bowed Judah's heart by gentle methods; a victorious king choosing to return by invitation, not force.
Keil: the subject is David; wise to appeal to his own tribe, but unjust to swear the command to the traitor Amasa.
Clarke: distrust breeds distrust; confidence placed in the suspected excites their confidence in return.

sides.position: David, by kind messages and affectionate speeches
Amasa, using his interest with Judah (the Jewish commentators)

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott map the meeting: David on the east side of the Jordan ford near Jericho, Judah at Gilgal on the opposite bank.
Gill: from Mahanaim to the Jordan was about twenty-eight miles (Bunting).
Ellicott: Shimei was an influential time-server, disloyal at heart, who came only because David's was the winning cause; Keil settles the crossing details.
Matthew Henry: Ziba and Shimei, who abused David in flight, meet him now — a warning to make peace with the Son of David before his glory.
Clarke: Shimei's thousand men prove him a powerful chieftain.

themes: make peace while in the way

schools.summary: Gill: the thousand men were Shimei's soldiers, tenants, or neighbours brought as intercessors.
Wesley: Shimei brought them to show his interest and as witnesses of David's clemency; Ziba came from a like guilty conscience.
Ellicott: 'before the king' means to meet him; they dashed through the ford to prove their doubtful allegiance.
Ellicott corrects the tense — 'as he was coming over'; the meeting was east of Jordan, before the crossing; Keil frames Shimei's fall at the king's feet.
Gill canvasses the craft — a ferry, a bridge of boats, or men bearing the household over (Abarbinel).
Clarke: this is the first mention of such a ferry-boat; some think a bridge or raft.

themes: majesty and mercy at the river

schools.summary: Gill: 'impute not iniquity' asks not denial but pardon, for non-imputation of sin is in effect its forgiveness.
Matthew Henry: Shimei's public submission fit a public offence — he owns and aggravates his crime and begs the king not to take it to heart.
'House of Joseph' stands for all the tribes but Judah; the usage need not date the book late (cf. 1 Chronicles 5, Psalm 80).
Gill: Shimei's confession bears marks of true repentance and an earnest of future fidelity.
Clarke: the confession was all he could do; only his later conduct could prove it; Wesley explains Benjamin reckoned under Joseph's standard.

themes: first to submit, first to claim mercy

schools.summary: Gill: Abishai is the same man who would have beheaded the cursing Shimei in the day of David's flight (2 Samuel 16:9).
Matthew Henry: Abishai consulted David's supposed feelings, not his interest; princes must arm against temptations to severity.
Keil sets the pardon of Shimei against the still less justifiable dealing with Mephibosheth to follow.

themes: zeal for revenge dressed as justice
law's letter vs. the day of grace

schools.summary: Ellicott: the word for 'adversaries' is literally Satan — David names his zealous kinsmen accusers.
Matthew Henry: those who advise us to wrong are really Satans; honour, joy, and received mercy all incline David to forgive.
Wesley: to be adversaries is to push the king to acts against his interest; forgiven of his own sin, he will not be revengeful.

themes: counsel of revenge as an adversary

schools.summary: Ellicott: viewed politically, David's oath was rash, tying his hands against a thorough traitor and bequeathing the case to Solomon (1 Kings 2).
Gill: the oath promised only that Shimei should not die this day by David's hand or sword.
Matthew Henry: pardon sealed with an oath, yet bound to good behaviour — Shimei reserved to show the government's justice as now its clemency.

themes: a rash oath kept to the letter

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: unwashed feet, untrimmed beard, and soiled clothes were signs of deep mourning (Ezekiel 24:17); he came down from Jerusalem.
Matthew Henry: the day of return was a day of account; Mephibosheth's unbroken mourning proved his loyalty and rebuked sensual ease in calamity.
Gill: 'son of Saul' means grandson; his neglected state marks him a true and obstinate mourner (with Wesley).
Clarke: his self-neglect was the fullest proof of attachment and refuted Ziba's slander.

themes: loyalty that keeps faith in absence

schools.summary: Ellicott: David's gentle question shows a doubt of Ziba's slander the moment he sees Mephibosheth; Keil fixes the clause — Jerusalem's people came to meet the king.
Gill: Mephibosheth had stayed near Jerusalem, unable through lameness to go meet the king as others did.
Wesley: he had continued near Jerusalem because he could not go to meet David.

themes: lameness at the heart of the case

schools.summary: Ellicott: the two provision-laden asses were Mephibosheth's own; Keil defends the plain Hebrew — a thing done by one need not be done with his own hands (Genesis 22:3).
Gill: Ziba, ordered to saddle an ass, went off with it and another himself, stranding his lame master.
Wesley: Ziba deceived him by carrying away the ass he had bidden him saddle.

themes: the plain truth of the wronged

schools.summary: The slander was that he stayed hoping for the kingdom; 'as an angel of God' means the king sees all as it is (2 Samuel 14:17).
Clarke: Mephibosheth states his case without guile and rests wholly in whatever the king determines.
Gill: the calumny that he eyed the throne had no foundation whatever.

themes: faith, not flattery, in 'an angel of God'

schools.summary: Gill: 'men of death' — either worthy of death or with lives lying at the king's mercy, as princes dealt with a rival house.
Keil: having been set at the king's table (2 Samuel 9:7,11), he has no right to cry any more.
Wesley: before the king's tribunal his life, not merely his estate, had been in David's power.
Matthew Henry: we were all dead men before God, yet seated at his table — how little reason to complain.

themes: from death to the king's table

schools.summary: Ellicott: the 'divide the land' ruling was a weak and unjust compromise; David should have reversed the grant and punished Ziba.
Clarke and Wesley: the division merely restored the original arrangement (2 Samuel 9:10), annulling Ziba's grant, though it left the traitor too much.
Matthew Henry: David feared or loved Ziba too much to do justice on a false witness (Deuteronomy 19:18-19); he was in a forgiving humour.

debates.question: How should David's 'divide the land' ruling be judged?

sides.position: a weak and unjust compromise — Ziba should have been punished, Mephibosheth fully righted
a just reinstatement of the original settlement, annulling the rash grant to Ziba

schools.summary: Gill: Mephibosheth was content to let Ziba keep the whole estate, the king's safe return outweighing all.
Matthew Henry: he drowns all care for his estate in joy at the king's return — a good man bears private loss while Israel is in peace.

themes: seeking the King, not his gifts
typology of the Son of David

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Barzillai, most generous of all in David's distress, escorts the king over Jordan seeking nothing — honour to whom honour is due (Romans 13:7).
Gill: Barzillai came down from Rogelim, his dwelling, to the Jordan.

themes: a large estate and a larger heart

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'provided of sustenance' = fed him, the same word David uses in return, service repaid manifold; Keil gives Barzillai's answer in full.
Gill: fourscore years was ten beyond the common term, a very great age.
Clarke: Barzillai's help at desperate Mahanaim proved his sincerity; David's offer was noble and liberal.
Gill: David meant not only to bring Barzillai over Jordan but on to Jerusalem, to his own table.
Keil reads the invitation straight into Barzillai's reply — 'how long have I to live?'
Matthew Henry: David sought his company and counsel and a chance to repay kindness, accepting it as a favour, not a debt.
Keil: Barzillai measures his fitness for court by his failing senses — 'how many days of the years of my life?'
Gill: this is Barzillai's reply to the king's grateful proposal.
Matthew Henry: the wisdom of age turns from the court's business and diversions alike.
Clarke and Wesley: an affecting picture of an infirm old man, dull of ear and taste, and a window into an Eastern court's vocal music.
Gill: Barzillai was full fourscore years, very little under or over.
Matthew Henry: the old should learn of Barzillai to be dead to the delights of sense and make a virtue of necessity.

themes: dead to the delights of sense

schools.summary: Gill: Barzillai would go only a little way past Jordan, then return to his city.
Matthew Henry: he lessens his own service and magnifies the king's reward — a figure of the saints amazed at grace's disproportion (Matthew 25:37).
Chimham was Barzillai's son (1 Kings 2:7); David likely gave him a house on his own estate by Bethlehem (Jeremiah 41:17).
Gill: Barzillai would turn back after seeing the king a little way on his journey.
Matthew Henry: it becomes the old to think much of dying — 'die in my nest' — yet Barzillai passes the honour to his son.
Gill: David admitted Chimham in his father's place.
Matthew Henry: David leaves the reward open — 'whatsoever thou shalt ask, I will do' — power's excellency being the capacity to do good.

themes: reward lavished on the benefactor's house

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: the king kisses Barzillai in farewell (Ruth 1:9) and he returns home; 'the people' means those faithful to David.
Clarke: the kiss was the token of farewell friendship; the blessing a prayer, probably a prophetic benediction.
Gill: all the people with David went over the Jordan.
All Judah and half of Israel escort the king to Gilgal; the other tribes had not had time or notice to join, sparking the quarrel.
Matthew Henry: the Israelite elders came too late for the first entrance, were put out of humour, and began a quarrel that damped the day's joy.
Wesley: Judah came entirely, Israel by halves — the occasion of this contention and of Sheba's coming sedition.
Israel, annoyed at being anticipated, charges Judah with 'stealing' the king; the old Judah-Ephraim jealousy, which will one day split the kingdom.
Gill: these were the principal men who had not crossed with David, meeting him on the road.
Wesley: to 'steal' the king was to convey him over hastily, with all his men, without their concurrence.

themes: a good work turned to grievance

schools.summary: Judah pleads nearness of kin and denies any gain — perhaps a taunt to the Benjamites over Saul's favouritism (1 Samuel 22:7); the whole dispute testifies to David's fair government.
Clarke and Wesley: Judah acted from loyalty, not advantage, having sought and gained nothing, but only discharged their duty.

themes: a good cause in a bad spirit

schools.summary: Israel claims 'ten parts' as ten tribes to Judah's one, and more right in David; the count omits Simeon or reckons Joseph as one.
Clarke: Judah's words had more weight and reason, yet Israel's greater guilt as first promoters of the revolt could not be shaken; Wesley: Israel answered with greater fierceness.
Matthew Henry: right and reason urged with fierceness displease God; a soft answer turns away wrath.

debates.question: Whose claim to the king was the stronger, Judah's or Israel's?

sides.position: Judah — nearness of kin, and words with more weight and reason
Israel — ten tribes to one, the far greater part of David's subjects, and first to move for his return
